speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 6 on August 29, 1996, of the interview of survivor Jack Arnel. Mr. Arnel, you've just described how the American Army has put you in an ambulance and taken you to a hospital called Sankt--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ottilien.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ottilien.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I just want to mention to you that when we came to Sankt Ottilien-- when the first ambulances brought us to Sankt Ottilien, there was no room. The, the-- all the halls and all the rooms were all taken up with the German wounded from the war. And so the American captain that was in charge over there, he took two soldiers with him. And he went up to a big hall where-- there were-- must have been 80 or 100 beds, a tremendous hall, like. And he opened the doors-- double door. He put on the machine gun right in front. And he said, I'm giving you just a few hours. Everybody's to get the hell out of here. And the nurses and everybody had to carry those that couldn't walk. And those that could walk, they were all put out onto the grass in the-- in front of it-- in front of the building. And then they started bringing in those survivors in, in there. And then gradually-- we weren't allowed to eat much. They had to break us in gradually. And after two months there, the strength came back to us. We were able to function normally, so to speak-- that is, on the physical end of it. And from there, they took us to the Feldafing Displaced Persons Camp, called a DP camp-- Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Feldafing?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-E-L-D-A-F-I-N-G, which is between Munich and, and Garmisch. It's in Bavaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went there with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my father, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Feldafing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Feldafing, we came-- we were liberated, actually, was-- May 1, 1945 is when we were totally liberated by the American Army. And we came to the United States in June of 1949. So we actually spent there, like, four years in the-- in the Displaced Persons Camp Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just-- I worked in the kitchen. They gave me a job to work in the kitchen to cook the food, me and a lot of other people. It was a big, big kitchen there. And we cooked the food for all the people that lived in the camp, Feldafing-- DP Camp Feldafing. And that was my job in the beginning. But something happened-- something wonderful happened to us. Of course, all the people that were there were all survivors, individuals who had lost all of their families, all of their parents and, and sisters and brothers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Europe was in, in ruins. And Munich was bombed out. The trains were going very slowly. There was no normal train ride or any-- any which way. And somehow, people started to travel from place to place. And in Munich, they formulated already a place of gathering for survivors. And they had started making lists of survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one man came to Feldafing. I met him accidentally. And we started talking. He says, what's your name? I says, my name is Jascha Aronowicz from Vilna. He says, give me your name again? I says, Jascha Aronowicz from Vilna. He said, did you have a mother by the name of Chaja and a sister-- her name was Sonja? I says, yes. He says, did you know that they are alive? I says, they are alive? Where are they? And he tells me that they are in-- he left them in Lodz, Poland-- that he knows for sure that they are alive. So I says, please, wait. And I immediately went, and I got my father. I brought him to him. And he verified it again to my father. And it was such a thrill to know that out of millions and millions, here is a family, a whole family, that can get together-- an immediate family, one out of millions. And he asked him again, where are they? He says, well, I left them in Lodz. He says, I know they are alive. I know that they're alive. Then we didn't know what to do and how to go about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But like a week later, another man came. And this man came-- he came from Lodz to Czechoslovakia and came. And he was-- also verified the fact that he met my sister and he's-- he met my, my, my mother. And he says, they know that we are alive because after the liberation, some people, after awhile they found their way to Poland. And they took some lists of them of survivors. And they found our names in the list, that we are alive. And they knew where we were in Feldafing. It was on the list over there. And they sent letters. But, of course, letters took, took three months to get anywheres at that time. It wasn't a normal way.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- this second man said that my mother and my sister are stuck in Czechoslovakia, in Prague. They had no money. And they can't pay any of the guys to take-- go through the borders.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no way of going through the borders. You get caught. You get arrested and put in jail. And they had to steal their way through it at night. And they are stuck. And my father was beside himself. Well, I have to do something about it. And he said-- my father decided that the next morning, he is going to Czechoslovakia. He'll find his way somehow, same way as others traveled. So will he. And so he took his few little things in the morning. And he went, and he made a plan. And then later on, he told me what happened. He went from Feldafing to Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the Munich station, he consulted with a German railroad worker over there. And he said-- told him the story about it. He said, I got to get to Prague. How do I get to Prague? I mean, what do I do now?

speaker: interviewee
text: The man advised him, don't travel during the day. Travel at night only. Get on freight trains that going [GERMAN], meaning east. And you got to find out where they're going. That's the only way you're going to get safely without being arrested. You may be able to go through the borders like that, too. Well, then, he had a whole day to kill yet before getting on a train going east. So he decided to go to the barbershop, take a haircut. And then he went to [GERMAN], which is a street where the Jew-- the, the survivors have formed a committee there. And people-- travelers were going back and forth. And it was a center for survivors over there at that time. And my father figured he'd walk in there and see what's doing. And as he was walking-- there were a lot of people there, with their suitcases and bundles, and all that-- travelers, back and forth. And as he was walking there, he found my mother and my sister, sitting on the-- on their bundles. And they-- the gathering between them was unbelievable. Everybody, they started screaming and yelling. And everybody surrounded them, you know. He would have missed them if he would have gone on to Czechoslovakia, had to go through the borders and try to find them. Could have been arrested, he could, by the Russians or by the Polacks or by the Czechs. And-- but thank God he was able-- he, he found them.

speaker: interviewer
text: He brought them to Feldafing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, here's what happened. I worked in the kitchen, as I told you. The entire day, I, I had mixed emotions. I didn't know. I was nervous. And I was-- something was happening to me. I had a sixth sense that my father is coming back. It was telling me that he was coming back. Somehow, I felt it in, in me, that he's coming back. And so I was supposed to quit at 5 o'clock. Everybody left the kitchen. But I stayed. I was afraid to leave the kitchen. I stayed on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some friends came by and said, what are you doing here? Why aren't you leaving the place? I says, no, I have to stay. There's something up or something. And around 9 o'clock in the evening, a group of, of people came running into the kitchen, saying, guess what? Your mother and your sister is here! Your mother and your sister is here! And, and, and for a moment, I broke out in a sweat. And I, I didn't know what it is-- the first thing I says, and where is my father? I thought that he-- I didn't-- I didn't know that he found them in there. He says, he's here, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, pandemonium-- I lit-- ran. And the entire camp of hundreds and hundreds of people have surrounded the building. It was such an emotional gathering. They were liberated by the Russian Army. And that's how they wound up in Lodz. And we were liberated by the American Army. And it was one out of millions of families that have-- had been separated, went through all the hell, and have found each other after the-- after the war. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They stayed with you in Feldafing, then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we stayed in--

speaker: interviewer
text: --all of you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: --together till--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. My father did some travel. He did-- we got back into the fur business a little bit. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we immediately registered to come to the United States. We wanted to, to leave that forsaken land. And could not get to go to any of their schools or anything like that, so we just stayed in the camp, in Feldafing. Of course, the Jewish organizations sent us all kinds of things, the clothing, the food. The Joint, the HIAS helped us. My father had friends, furriers, in the United States, in New York, that went there before the war. He made contact with them. And they sent us affidavits that made us-- enabled us to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm, so you got your permission to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got our, our permission to come to the United States. And my father's friends, they guaranteed that we'll not be a burden to the government, that they will provide us with jobs, that we will support ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you travel here?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, we came by ship. They-- in fact, we left Germany at Bremerhaven. It was in June of 1949. And there was a, a troop transport ship by the name of And they loaded us onto the ship, quite a lot of us. And it was a-- quite an awful journey because people weren't used to traveling on, on ships. And the waves were so big, a lot of people took sick-- seasickness, and all that. But nothing bothered me. I made friends with the crew. And they, they fed me. They-- come for more, come for more. And, and we finally arrived in Boston Harbor. In Boston Harbor, the, the highest committee in the Joint Committee, they greeted us and accepted us. And they put us on trains towards New York. And we arrived in New York. I can still remember the trip on the-- on the train towards New York, with all the glitter and all the lights, which-- I've never seen anything like that before in my life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] When we got off, I was ready to go down on my knees and kiss the ground. [CRYING] [DRINKS] We have arrived in New York. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Jewish organizations have helped us quite a bit, provided us with an apartment, provided us with jobs. I have tried to get myself into a normal mode of life. But I knew that I was very much behind-- no education, no formal education. I've missed all that in my youth. I came here. I was 20 years old. But four years in Feldafing without any schooling, the suffering during the war has made its mark. And I had to fight it. And I had to get around it. I've thrown myself into working. I have attended evening schools for many, many years. And I worked in the Garment Center. I married my wife, Sheila. We had three sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in 1954, I believe. I better be accurate on that. [LAUGHTER] In 1954, March-- I better not say it. Somehow, I, I can't concentrate--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have three sons. My oldest son is Curt Arnel. My middle son is Marc Arnel. And my youngest son is Jeffrey.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work did you do in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I was mainly involved in the Garment Center. See, the first job that I was given by the HIAS, they put me in a factory where they were producing some garments. And I learned how to do some spreading fabrics and cutting, and all that. And while I was doing that, I was going to Washington Irving Evening High School. I graduated from there. And it took a lot of effort because I worked six days a week. And five nights a week, I was running to school. I had this big, big drive to-- I had to catch up. I am very behind, and all that. And it took a number of years. And then, when I graduated from the evening high school, I went to Fashion Institute of Technology. And I went there for a number of years. And I got all the courses needed to make a livelihood, to stand on my own two feet. And it was so wonderful, the liberty, the freedom to choose what I want to do, and to choose-- and the school was there. And, and anything I wanted-- I wasn't used to it. And I'm most grateful, I'm most appreciative to the United States and all they did on our behalf-- and the Jewish organizations. The, the-- finally, during the years, I worked hard. I moved away from New York, came back to New York. And all in all, I was in-- became a manager. And I dealt with the big [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] chain stores, representing my, my companies that I work for as a production manager. And life was very sweet and very good for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you retired now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am retired now, a couple of years. And I must state, though, that marrying my wife, which-- she was an American. She did not-- she's not a survivor from the other side. But her parents are from my hometown, from Vilna. And it was quite-- with her love and understanding-- [CRYING] and she was a great help to me to overcome some of the fears and some of the leftover from the Second War, some of the hardships that still are looming in my mind, some of these thoughts.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very difficult. I try not to impose it on any of my children or anybody else. It's a difficult walk. I know I have suffered an awful lot. But is it fair to impose it on others? Do you have any comments that you'd like to make about all your experiences, about these activities today, or any message?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can say this, that what has happened to the Jewish people during Second World War I struggle to believe could happen again, not necessarily to the Jewish people. I think that if the world does not remember what has happened during that time and do the right things-- I'm talking the entire world-- the entire world population-- it will come to haunt some people somewheres. We have to be alert. We have to tell the story to all the students. As uncomfortable as it is, they must have knowledge of it, of what has transpired, the suffering that one people has forced on others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not only Jews suffered. The Russians have lost 20 million people during Second World War. How many millions should be slaughtered? How many people should be killed? When do we wake up and say, enough?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let us concentrate on helping each other to formulate a life of happiness. But we have to stand on guard all the time. It has happened. It manifested itself again in Yugoslavia-- former Yugoslavia. The racial cleansing there has manifested itself in Africa, one tribe against another. I think that the powers of the world have to get together, to one way or the other-- and to root out and do something about it, wherever it manifests itself, this hate. Because the consequences are just too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mr. Arnel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, sir. I also want to say thank you so much to Mr. Spielberg for affording me the opportunity to tell my story. [CRYING] God bless him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you again.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture that a friend of mine had and found after the war. And it shows my father's fur shop before the war. And you have on the left two of his workers. Then my father is in the middle. And to the-- on the right side-- on the left side of my father, actually, is my cousin Srolik. And then there's another worker over there. Srolik was killed during the Aktions by the Germans before they-- even the ghetto was, was-- has been established. This picture is a birthday party before the war, approximately 1937. And in the middle is my friend Boris Trahtenberg, with the tie on and a white shirt. And right next to him is me. I must have been there, eight and nine years old. The rest are cousins of my friend. It was taken at the residence of my friend. And the-- this was his aunt on the left side, there. And looking at that little boy was his uncle, his father's brother. They all died in the war. They were all killed by the Nazis. Only Boris and I out of this picture survived. This is a picture of myself after the war, a few months after I arrived to Feldafing. And I took a picture with my clothing that I still had-- the striped garb. And I have a patch with my number, which was 80984. That was given-- assigned to me, actually, by the Nazis when I arrived to Camp I, Dachau. This is a composite of pictures of the Displaced Persons camp after the war, where I spent four years waiting for my visa to come to the United States. And this reflects-- right in the middle there is a memorial to the-- to, to the people that were killed in that particular area. And we show some of the-- of the places we lived in. And there is this lake, Starnberger See, where we used to go swimming over there. And generally showing the--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the Feldafing camp, in other words.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Feldafing camp--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. In the right-hand corner up above, I think we were celebrating the establishment of the-- of Israel. This is my sister, Sonja, taken in Feldafing in December of 1945. Here I am with my mother and my father. This picture was taken also in Feldafing in 1949, just prior to arriving to the United States. This is the ship brought us to the United States. It was the And we were-- we were-- fell in love with this ship to-- that brought us to the new freedom. My father and mother, just about a short while after we arrived to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture, I believe, was taken in Brooklyn-- I believe 1949. This is our wedding picture, my darling Sheila. This was 1956 at our wedding. This is a picture of the town of Kaufering, where they-- during the war, Camp Number V was there. And I was transferred there. We were there until the very end of the war. We traveled through Europe on a vacation, my wife and I and some friends of ours. And we came to came to Kaufering to see if there is any kind of a memorial or anything there. We couldn't find anything whatsoever. And we went to the police department if there is anything pertaining to the concentration camp that was there. And they said they had no knowledge about it. And they gave me a telephone number to call the historian of the town. He may be able to enlighten me. And when I called, I was told that the historian died five years ago. And I showed my wife and my friends where we had arrived. And with disgust, I just left the place. And I don't want to see it in the rest of my life. This picture is my immediate family. Starting from left is my son, Dr. Marc Arnel; my wife, Sheila and myself over there; and then there is Lisa, which is the wife of my youngest son, Jeff, who is right behind her, Dr. Jeff Arnel. And we have also Bobbie. Bobbie is the wife of my oldest son, Curt Arnel. And this is my, my joy in life. And I love them dearly. And talking about joy, this is my granddaughter, Lauren. And she was born in on May 8, 1949-- I'm sorry, 1989.

speaker: interviewer
text: She--

speaker: interviewee
text: And May 8 is a very significant date to us. May 8 is when the World War II ended and the German Nazis capitulated. And so it is very significant to me to have a grandchild born on that particular day-- celebration of the destruction of the Nazi machinery. This is my grandson, the newest arrival, Spencer Dean Arnel. And he is four months old at this time. And he's the son of Lisa and Jeff. And needless to say, how much we love him. This picture, I am here with Poet Laureate [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Elie Wiesel, which was taken at the opening of the Holocaust Museum in Washington, DC, on April 22, 1993.
